bibliographic.citation: Thomas Hearne, ed., Robert of Gloucester's Chronicle, in The Works of Thomas Hearne, vols. I–II (London: printed for Samuel Bagster, 1810).
bibliographic.pages: Chronicle pp. 1–571; subsequent apparatus and indexes through p. 792
bibliographic.series: The Works of Thomas Hearne, vols. I–II
bibliographic.title: Robert of Gloucester's Chronicle

entities.label: Samuel Bagster's 1810 reset reprint of Hearne's Robert of Gloucester's Chronicle
notes: The series title reads The Works of Thomas Hearne, printed for Samuel Bagster in the Strand, 1810, while the following old title page reproduces the 1724 Oxford wording.

The text is newly typeset rather than a photographic facsimile. Printed p. 364 ends with 'Printed by Mercier and Chervet, No. 32, Little Bartholomew Close, London,' a diagnostic present in the XML and absent from the 1724 p. 364.
Volume I contains the prefatory material and Chronicle pp. 1–364. Volume II resumes at p. 367 after a half-title and contains the Chronicle through p. 571; appendices begin on p. 572.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Hearne, ed., Robert of Gloucester's Chronicle, Transcrib'd, and now first publish'd, from a MS. in the Harleyan Library, 2 vols. (Oxford: printed at the Theater, 1724).
bibliographic.pages: Chronicle pp. 1–571, with preface, appendix, glossary, index, and related matter
bibliographic.title: Robert of Gloucester's Chronicle

entities.label: Thomas Hearne's 1724 edition of Robert of Gloucester's Chronicle
notes: Hearne says he abandoned a Bodleian copy, transcribed the D'Ewes/Harley manuscript supplied through Richard Mead as his base text, and appended the entire Cotton continuation where Harley ends.
He compared the substantially altered College of Arms manuscript supplied through John Bridges, omitted most of its prose and interpolated material, and marked selected observations and readings Ar.
Hearne's statements about author, manuscript age, completeness, and intended ending are historical editorial claims, not adopted uncritically as modern work facts.

dateStatements.label: production
dateStatements.value: first quarter of the fifteenth century (1400–1424)

holding.notes: The Robert of Gloucester Chronicle occupies ff. 4r–161r.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 201
notes: The British Library explicitly records its use by Hearne and notes that it supplies text missing in Cotton Caligula A XI.
The physical manuscript extends beyond the work component only minimally, but the complete codex—not just work folios—would be the correct facsimile target.

physicalDescription.extent: i + 162 folios, with an unfoliated front paper flyleaf and two rear paper flyleaves
physicalDescription.format: codex; Middle English rhyming chronicle in Gothic script
notes: Blank parchment precedes f. 4; f. 31 is misbound and should precede f. 30.
The manuscript was rebound by the British Museum in 1951 and was produced in England.

dateStatements.label: production
dateStatements.notes: This is the British Library's current component date. It is recorded separately from Hearne's historical opinion and the internally argued post-1297 or post-1307 date of the long recension.
dateStatements.value: last quarter of the thirteenth century (1275–1300)

holding.notes: The Chronicle occupies ff. 3r–13v and 15r–168v; the component record is labeled ff. 3–13, 15–169.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A XI
notes: The codex also contains the Short Metrical Chronicle at f. 14, Piers Plowman B at ff. 170–286, and De vita monachorum at ff. 287–288; these adjacent works are not part of CME00022.
Hearne's printed continuation head cites Cotton Library, Caligula A. XI, f. 130b at the point where Harley ends.

physicalDescription.extent: 288 folios in the whole composite codex
notes: The manuscript was damaged in the 1731 Cotton Library fire and rebound by the British Museum in 1951.
The Robert of Gloucester component is cataloged as the first version and imperfect.
physicalDescription.support: principally parchment, with paper at ff. 1, 75, and 81

dateStatements.label: production
dateStatements.value: fifteenth century; Fisher describes a mid-fifteenth-century parchment codex

holding.notes: Consultation is by appointment in the College of Arms record room.

entities.label: College of Arms Arundel MS 58
notes: The AIM25 description calls the text a History of England that remodels Robert of Gloucester's metrical chronicle with interpolations and additions from the Brut, Geoffrey of Monmouth, William of Malmesbury, and other sources, continuing to 1332.
It is a substantively remodeled derivative witness and comparison source, not Hearne's base manuscript and not a simple upstream copy of every encoded line.
Fisher's codicological discussion identifies three scribal portions and analyzes the manuscript's substantial prose remodeling and interpolations.

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether complete existing photography, legacy microfilm, or unindexed digital objects exist for Harley 201 and Cotton Caligula A XI.
Ask the College of Arms whether complete photography of Arundel 58 exists and obtain written cost, retention, publication, and reuse terms.

If new photography is required, request every physical surface of each complete codex—bindings, boards, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, and adjacent works—not only the Chronicle components.
After obtaining complete objects, map CME00022's exact work folios and source-splice passages separately with image/canvas locators and first/end deep links.
notes: No manuscript image entered the cache because no complete public deliverable was verified.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable facsimiles be obtained for Harley MS 201, Cotton MS Caligula A XI, and College of Arms Arundel MS 58?

next_steps: Review post-2005 work on the Robert self-reference, recension chronology, and manuscript stemma.
Seek the British Library's current codicological basis for the Cotton component date.
Retain separate, explicitly attributed claims rather than forcing one normalized date.
openQuestions.question: Who composed and revised the two recensions, and how should the long recension's internally argued post-1297 or post-1307 date be reconciled with Cotton Caligula A XI's current 1275–1300 catalog date?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for item-level production metadata, source-copy provenance, and any source XML package.
Compare representative XML spellings, notes, and page breaks against the cached 1810 scan while preserving the already confirmed reset-edition boundary.
Keep the 2016-09-23 TEMPHEAD entry scoped to review rather than initial encoding.
openQuestions.question: What source copy, initial keying method and date, correction history, and downloadable-source terms apply to the U-M CME00022 encoding?

next_steps: Compare the endings of every long-recension witness and modern critical editions.
Inspect Cotton's terminal collation and fire damage directly or in a complete facsimile.
Distinguish Hearne's historical conjecture from recoverable witness evidence and from Fisher's statement that no transmitted complete ending survives.
openQuestions.question: How much of the long recension's ending is lost, and did any medieval witness preserve the intended continuation through 1272 that Hearne believed had existed?

reviewNotes: The abstract cataloging subject is the Middle English long recension; the witnesses, Hearne's hybrid edition, Bagster's reset reprint, and the digital encodings are separate lineage layers.
The Mercier and Chervet colophon at printed p. 364 occurs in the 1810 scan and encoded XML but not at p. 364 of the 1724 first edition, confirming the immediate reset-reprint source.
Generic U-M collection production statements were not transferred to this item. The XML documents a 2016-09-23 review, not the initial keying date or method.
Complete facsimile coverage means the provider's complete exposed physical scan object; the work-bearing portions are recorded separately in local_copies[].work_portion.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00022 XML represents a University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding keyed from Samuel Bagster's 1810 reset reprint of Thomas Hearne's 1724 two-volume edition, despite reproducing the 1724 title page. Hearne transcribed his main text from British Library Harley MS 201, appended the continuation from British Library Cotton MS Caligula A XI where Harley ends, and selectively collated College of Arms Arundel MS 58 for readings marked Ar. The encoded selection is the old title page and Chronicle pp. 1–571; it omits the 1810 series title, Hearne's publisher's preface and contents, and the appendix, glossary, index, and other back matter. Complete public scans of both 1810 volumes and both 1724 volumes are cached with scan maps, as is the principal research thesis. No complete public facsimile of any of the three manuscripts was verified on 2026-07-12; current catalog and mediated consultation routes are recorded instead.

catalogSummary.author: Robert of Gloucester (attributed; identity and extent of responsibility uncertain)
catalogSummary.date: Long recension after 1297, perhaps after 1307; exact composition history uncertain
catalogSummary.region: Middle English work associated linguistically with the Worcestershire–Gloucestershire area; exact place of composition unknown
catalogSummary.title: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester (long recension)

catalogingSubject.label: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester (long recension)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the Middle English verse long recension traditionally attributed to Robert of Gloucester, as represented by Thomas Hearne's hybrid text from British Library Harley MS 201 and Cotton MS Caligula A XI. It excludes the shorter recension, the physically distinct manuscripts and their adjacent works, the internally remodeled prose-and-verse text in College of Arms Arundel MS 58, Hearne's English and Latin apparatus, the 1724 and 1810 printed books as material editions, and the U-M and repository encodings as digital layers.

contentParts.label: Harley-based main text: Brutus through the Empress Matilda's escape from Oxford
notes: The part boundary describes Hearne's witness splice, not a recovered medieval division.
contentParts.summary: The proem, geography and praise of England, legendary British history, Roman and Anglo-Saxon rule, the Danish and Norman periods, and the chronicle through the point in Stephen's reign where Harley MS 201 ends.

contentParts.label: Cotton-based continuation: Stephen through events of 1271

locators.value: chronicle at ff. 3r–13v and 15r–168v; Hearne's printed head cites f. 130b at the splice

notes: Cotton Caligula A XI is imperfect and also contains unrelated works; only its Robert of Gloucester component belongs to this source role.
contentParts.summary: The continuation supplied where Harley ends, extending the history through Henry II and his successors, Magna Carta and its aftermath, Henry III, Simon de Montfort and baronial conflict, and the murder of Henry of Almain before the surviving text breaks off.

assertion.notes: The lower bound derives from internal historical references analyzed by Fisher. The stronger post-1307 proposal remains qualified, and no single completion year is asserted.

dateStatements.display: The long recension was composed or revised after 1297 and perhaps after 1307

assertion.notes: This is the current catalog date of the physical witness. Its tension with the internally argued post-1297 date of the long recension remains unresolved rather than silently harmonized.

dateStatements.display: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A XI is currently cataloged to the last quarter of the thirteenth century

scope.description: The long-recension Cotton witness used for Hearne's continuation

assertion.notes: This is the first publication identified in the inspected edition history; it is not a claim that no earlier excerpt ever circulated in print.

dateStatements.display: First printed by Thomas Hearne at Oxford in 1724, in two volumes
Legendary foundation of Britain through events of 1271; the transmitted long text breaks off before a complete ending

extent.description: The repository encoding has 94 LG elements and 12,198 L elements in total. Excluding one L nested inside an editorial NOTE leaves 12,197 direct work-text lines: 9,679 in the Harley-based main segment and 2,518 in the Cotton-based continuation. It has 570 PB elements overall (569 in BODY), two BODY DIV1 elements, and one supplement DIV2. These are encoding-specific counts, not a claim about canonical medieval lineation or authorial sectioning.

formStatements.description: A Middle English historical chronicle in rhyming couplets. The repository XML contains 12,197 direct work-text L elements in 94 LG elements, apart from one line nested inside an editorial note.

assertion.notes: The chronicle compiles and reshapes earlier historical traditions rather than serving as a neutral event record.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin, Anglo-Norman, and modern English occur in Hearne's notes and editorial matter but are not modeled as intrinsic languages of the medieval verse work.
The preferred title retains the traditional association with Robert of Gloucester but explicitly qualifies authorship and fixes the cataloging boundary at the long recension.
The work's uncertain composition history, witness dates, Hearne's 1724 edition, Bagster's 1810 reset, and the U-M encoding are not collapsed into one date or artifact.
Harley MS 201 supplies Hearne's main text, Cotton MS Caligula A XI supplies the continuation, and Arundel MS 58 supplies selected apparatus readings; these are distinct source roles.
The 12,197-line and two-part description is an encoding-based navigational summary, not an assertion of canonical medieval lineation or authorial books.
No complete public facsimile of any of the three manuscripts was verified on 2026-07-12.

next_steps: Consult post-2005 scholarship on recension chronology, the Robert self-reference, and Cotton paleography.
Ask the British Library whether the component date reflects a recent codicological reassessment and whether a narrower date is supportable.
Keep the traditional Robert attribution qualified and the two date layers distinct until the conflict is resolved.
openQuestions.question: Who composed and revised the Chronicle's two recensions, and how should the internally argued post-1297 date of the long recension be reconciled with the British Library's current 1275–1300 dating of Cotton Caligula A XI?

next_steps: Ask the British Library about unindexed photography or digitization for both manuscripts and request complete physical-object coverage if images must be ordered.
Ask the College of Arms about existing full photography of Arundel MS 58, costs, retention, and reuse terms.
Map work portions only after complete-object coverage and exact folio/canvas sequences are verified.
notes: The lineage manifest records the dated search scope and mediated physical-access routes; no manuscript image was cached.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable digital facsimiles be obtained for Harley MS 201, Cotton MS Caligula A XI, and College of Arms Arundel MS 58?

next_steps: Ask the U-M Digital Collections team for item-level production metadata, copy provenance, and any downloadable source package.
Do not transfer generic collection-level manual-keying statements to this item without item evidence.
Keep 2016-09-23 scoped to the documented TEMPHEAD review rather than encoding creation.
openQuestions.question: What physical copy, initial keying date and method, correction history, and source-file terms apply to the U-M CME00022 encoding?

next_steps: Compare all long-recension witnesses and modern critical editions at the ending.
Separate Hearne's belief about an intended 1272 endpoint from recoverable witness evidence.
Inspect Cotton directly or in a complete facsimile for the missing-leaf sequence and terminal codicology.
openQuestions.question: How much text is missing from the end of the long recension and from Cotton Caligula A XI, and did any medieval copy ever preserve the intended ending through 1272?

assertion.notes: Fisher summarizes the original dialect as belonging to the Worcestershire/Gloucestershire area while cautioning against overprecise localization.
The conventional attribution is retained for discovery, not promoted to established authorship.

notes: The number of contributors and the relation between the shorter and longer recensions remain uncertain.

Hearne transcribed Harley MS 201 as his main text, appended the continuation from Cotton MS Caligula A XI where Harley ends, and introduced selected readings from College of Arms Arundel MS 58 marked Ar.

scope.description: The 1724 edition's base text and continuation, not authorship of the medieval work
Selected readings and observations from Arundel MS 58 in the printed apparatus

subjects.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
Henry III, baronial conflict, and the Second Barons' War

summaries.value: A Middle English verse history that opens with the geography and praise of England, begins Britain's past with Brutus, and carries a dynastic narrative through British, Roman, Anglo-Saxon, Danish, Norman, Angevin, and thirteenth-century rule. Its later sections give extended attention to the Norman Conquest, Stephen and Matilda, Henry III, Simon de Montfort, and baronial conflict. The transmitted long text ends incompletely during the aftermath of the murder of Henry of Almain in 1271.

Hearne's represented text has two source-defined segments: pp. 1–464 from Harley MS 201, ending during Stephen's reign as the Empress Matilda escapes Oxford, and pp. 465–571 from Cotton MS Caligula A XI, continuing into Henry III's reign and breaking off in the events of 1271. The boundary is explicit in the edition and XML but is not treated as an authorial book division.

The XML's title page names Hearne's 1724 publication, but page images and the colophon at printed p. 364 show that its immediate typographic source is Samuel Bagster's 1810 reset reprint. The medieval work, Hearne's 1724 edition, Bagster's 1810 reprint, and the digital encodings are therefore kept as distinct descriptive layers.

assertion.notes: The wording qualifies the traditional author attribution and distinguishes the longer recension represented by Hearne's source witnesses.

work.summary: A Middle English verse history that opens with the geography and praise of England, begins Britain's past with Brutus, and carries a dynastic narrative through British, Roman, Anglo-Saxon, Danish, Norman, Angevin, and thirteenth-century rule. Its later sections give extended attention to the Norman Conquest, Stephen and Matilda, Henry III, Simon de Montfort, and baronial conflict. The transmitted long text ends incompletely during the aftermath of the murder of Henry of Almain in 1271.
